FootNote: The Caribbean Court of Justice has two jurisdictions, an Appellate Jurisdiction and an Original Jurisdiction. In its Appellate Jurisdiction, the Court serves as the final court of appeal in civil and criminal matters for those countries of the Caribbean Community which are parties to the Agreement and have acceded to the appellate jurisdiction. In its Original Jurisdiction the Court is a court of first instance which applies rules of international law in respect of the interpretation and application of the Revised Treaty of Chaguaramas. At present the Court receives cases in its Appellate Jurisdiction from Barbados, Belize, Dominica, Guyana and Saint Lucia.

FootNote: The criteria used to define court levels take into account the job description and its hierarchy according to the Organic Code of the Judicial Function, since it is only possible to clearly identify the Constitutional Court and the Judicial Council. Supreme/constitutional courts: positions associated with the National Court. Higher-level courts: positions associated with the provincial level, criminal guarantees courts, criminal tribunals, or positions that do not belong to a specific judicial unit but operate at the first-level jurisdiction. Lower-level courts: positions associated with judicial units, without a specific denomination, at the first-level jurisdiction. The reference period for the data reported is January 2019.

Source: Distributivo Presupuestario de Remuneraciones y Nómina del Presupuesto General del Estado, proporcionado por el Ministerio de Economía y Finanzas (MEF)

Lower-level courts: positions associated with judicial units, without a specific denomination, at the first-level jurisdiction. The reference period for the data reported is January 2021.

FootNote: The Israeli Judicial system is comprised of three levels of courts: The Supreme Court, District Courts and Magistrates' Courts. The Supreme Court is the highest Court of the State of Israel. It also serves as the High Court of Justice (HCJ), hearing petitions against various governmental authorities and tribunals as the first (and only) instance on some such matters. The President of the Supreme Court heads the entire judicial system. Justice Esther Hayut currently serves in this role. District Courts hear appeals and also serve as courts of first instance for certain civil, criminal and administrative cases. Judges of the National Labor Court are listed as District Court Judges (9 judges). Magistrates' Courts serve as courts of first instance for criminal and civil cases within their jurisdiction, and also act as traffic, municipal, family, juvenile and small claims courts. Family Court Judges and Regional Labor Court Judges are listed as Magistrates' Court Judges (71 judges). Please note: 1. One of the Registrars of the Supreme Court holds an appointment as a Magistrates' Court judge. 2. For legal reasons, the Courts Administration does not independently collect data regarding the proportions of people with disabilities within the judiciary. Such data can only be obtained through the National Insurance Institute.

The President of the Supreme Court heads the entire judicial system. Justice Esther Hayut currently serves in this role. District Courts hear appeals and also serve as courts of first instance for certain civil, criminal and administrative cases. Judges of the National Labor Court are listed as District Court Judges (9 judges). Magistrates' Courts serve as courts of first instance for criminal and civil cases within their jurisdiction, and also act as traffic, municipal, family, juvenile and small claims courts. Family Court Judges and Regional Labor Court Judges are listed as Magistrates' Court Judges (71 judges). Please note: 1. One of the Registrars of the Supreme Court holds an appointment as a Magistrates' Court judge. 2. For legal reasons, the Courts Administration does not independently collect data regarding the proportions of people with disabilities within the judiciary. Such data can only be obtained through the National Insurance Institute.

Professional Judges: Both full time and part time official legal professionals as of 31 December authorized to hear civil, criminal and other cases, including in appeal courts, and to make dispositions in a court of law, which have been recruited and are paid to practice as a judge. This category excludes non-professional judges such as lay judges and lay magistrates.

FootNote: Professional Judges: Both full time and part time official legal professionals as of 31 December authorized to hear civil, criminal and other cases, including in appeal courts, and to make dispositions in a court of law, which have been recruited and are paid to practice as a judge. Professional Judges: Both full time and part time official legal professionals as of 31 December authorized to hear civil, criminal and other cases, including in appeal courts, and to make dispositions in a court of law, which have been recruited and are paid to practice as a judge. FootNote: The statistical tables are structured by their compilers in such a way that data on constitutional courts and supreme courts are presented in the same field. Given that both the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Lithuania and the Supreme Court of Lithuania operate in Lithuania, data on these courts are presented separately, using the following abbreviations: CC – “Constitutional court” and SP – “Supreme court”. It should be noted that higher-level courts include the Constitutional Court of the Republic of Lithuania, the Supreme Court of Lithuania, the Court of Appeal of Lithuania, the Supreme Administrative Court of Lithuania, the regional administrative court and regional courts, accordingly “lower-level courts” include district courts.

We have used register-based data in order to report the number of judiciary personnel. We were not able to disaggregate by type of court. You will therefore only get the total numbers of personnel in the judiciary but they are disaggregated by type of position and for each socio-demographic characteristics, except for Disability status. Here you can read more about our register-based data: https://www.ssb.no/en/arbeid-og-lonn/sysselsetting/statistikk/sysselsetting-registerbasert, and here for immigrants: https://www.ssb.no/en/arbeid-og-lonn/sysselsetting/statistikk/sysselsetting-blant-innvandrere-registerbasert. The numbers we report are based on a data collection containing personnel in the industry '84.230: Justice and judicial activities' with the occupations '2611: Lawyers', '2612: Judges' or '2619: Legal professionals not elsewhere classified'. Read more about the classifications we use on industry (SIC 2007: https://www.ssb.no/klass/klassifikasjoner/6) and occupations (ISCO-08: https://www.ssb.no/klass/klassifikasjoner/7) in order to see what is included or not.

The Eastern Caribbean Supreme Court (ECSC) is an international tribunal of justice shared by Antigua & Barbuda, Anguilla, Dominica, Grenada, Montserrat, St. Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, St Vincent and the Grenadines, Territory of the Virgin Islands. The Eastern Caribbean Supreme Court consists of two divisions, a Court of Appeal and a High Court of Justice. The Court of Appeal judges are based at the Court’s Headquarters in Castries, Saint Lucia. The High Court Judges are each assigned to, and reside in, the various Member States.
